html:
Gerresheimer is a high-risk cleanup stock, but the market is still pricing it too much like an accounting zero and not enough like a strategic pharma-packaging franchise with a forced deleveraging catalyst.

The accounting problem is real. BaFin widened its audit, the 2025 annual report and Q1 statement are delayed, Gerresheimer was removed from the SDAX for late reporting, and the company itself now expects 2024 corrections of about EUR 35 million of revenue and EUR 24 million of adjusted EBITDA, plus EUR 220-240 million of mostly noncash impairments tied largely to Sensile Medical and Chicago moulded glass. That is not noise.

But the variant view is that this is a solvency scare around a still-valuable asset, not a permanent impairment of the core business. The company still guides 2026 revenue of EUR 2.3-2.4 billion, adjusted EBITDA margin of 18-19%, and moderately positive free cash flow. At roughly EUR 24.36 per share, about CHF 22.2 using EUR/CHF near 0.912, the equity value is only about EUR 840 million against guided 2026 EBITDA of roughly EUR 415-455 million. With about EUR 2.0 billion of net debt last reported, the stock is not optically cheap on leverage or near-term EPS, but it is still only about 6.3-6.8x guided EBITDA before any Centor sale benefit.

The key is that the balance sheet has a path. Creditors have extended the deadline for audited 2025 accounts to September 30, 2026 and waived key leverage covenants through Q3. That sharply lowers the near-term forced-equity-raise risk. The sale process for Centor has started with a double-digit number of interested parties, and management still expects closing before year-end. Centor is a U.S. prescription packaging asset, not the core injectable biologics and high-value syringe story. A sale at even a middling EUR 450-650 million would not magically make the company underlevered, but it would move Gerresheimer from distressed balance-sheet optionality toward a financeable pharma-packaging turnaround.

The core business remains better than the stock chart. Gerresheimer supplies vials, cartridges, ampoules, prefillable syringes, pens, auto-injectors, inhalers, closures, and medical devices. The most attractive part is not commodity glass. It is high-value containment and delivery for biologics, biosimilars, GLP-1s, oncology, ophthalmology, diabetes, and autoimmune drugs. Once a drug is approved with a validated primary container or device, switching suppliers is slow, regulated, expensive, and operationally risky. Gerresheimer’s own annual report describes injectables as about 90% of the global drug pipeline, and independent market data still point to roughly 10% annual growth in prefilled syringes into 2031.

What makes the setup unusually interesting is that almost nobody can own it cleanly right now. The audited accounts are missing. The index bid is gone. Retail sentiment in German forums is still anxious and skeptical despite the rebound. Yet sophisticated capital is not walking away: Active Ownership has been buying heavily around EUR 24-26, its effective exposure is reported near 16.85%, Goldman’s aggregate voting-rights position including instruments is around 20%, and Reuters reported that Silgan’s earlier non-binding approach was EUR 41 per share, more than double the then-current price. A strategic buyer looking at containers, devices, and customer approvals sees assets the public market is currently marking through a governance discount.

The base case should not assume heroic margins. It should assume 2026 EBITDA around EUR 430 million, Centor sold, net debt reduced toward EUR 1.4-1.6 billion, and post-sale EBITDA around EUR 360-385 million. At 8x post-sale EBITDA, still below premium medtech packaging valuations, equity value is roughly EUR 1.3-1.6 billion, or EUR 38-46 per share. Converted to CHF, that supports a base target around CHF 40.

The bull case is not that accounting was irrelevant. It is that the audit is ugly but finite, Centor sells well, the company proves positive free cash flow, and investors re-underwrite Gerresheimer as a simplified biologics packaging and drug-delivery supplier. If post-sale EBITDA is EUR 390-410 million and the cleaned-up company gets 9-10x EBITDA, the stock can trade around CHF 60-65. A renewed strategic bid would make that faster.

The bear case is dilution or loss of trust. If the 2025 audit finds broader revenue, inventory, or customer-contract issues, if the Centor sale fails, or if lenders only extend support in exchange for shareholder pain, the equity is a levered stub. A 5-6x multiple on impaired EBITDA with EUR 2 billion of debt can put the stock back near CHF 10.

At today’s approximate CHF 22.2 price, my targets are:

Bear: CHF 10, about 55% downside.

Base: CHF 40, about 80% upside.

Bull: CHF 64, about 188% upside.
